title: Basic leather care: cleaning and conditioning
subtitle: What different leathers need and why conditioning matters
excerpt: An explanation of how aniline, semi-aniline, and pigmented leathers differ in care needs, what happens when leather dries out, and how to choose a cleaner and conditioner for domestic upholstery.
sourceNotes: Leather Conservation Centre guidance (publicly available). Connolly Leather care guides. British Leather Confederation technical notes.

glossaryTerms.definition: Leather dyed with transparent dye that leaves the natural grain fully visible. No pigment coating is applied. The most natural-feeling leather: very soft, breathes well, darkens with use. Also the most absorbent and most easily stained.

Leather with a pigment coating on the surface, giving a uniform colour and a slightly plastic feel. More resistant to staining and fading than aniline; easier to clean; less breathable. The majority of modern mass-market upholstery leather is pigmented.

content.text: Leather is skin. It contains natural oils that keep it supple; when those oils are lost through everyday use and cleaning, the leather dries, stiffens, and eventually cracks. Cleaning removes surface grime; conditioning restores the oil balance. The two steps are different and both matter.
(also called full aniline or naked leather)

is dyed right through with no surface coating. It is the softest and most natural type but the most vulnerable to water, oils, and UV. Spills absorb within seconds. It requires a gentle pH-neutral cleaner and regular conditioning.
has a light protective coating over aniline dye. It retains much of the natural feel while offering more resistance to staining. It responds to the same care regime as aniline leather.
has a polymer coating that gives the uniform colour of most modern furniture leather. It is the most durable and easiest to clean but still benefits from conditioning.

Place a small drop of water on a hidden area. If it absorbs within a few seconds and darkens the surface, the leather is aniline or semi-aniline. If it beads and sits on the surface, it is pigmented. The supplier's documentation or care label should confirm the type.

Use a pH-neutral leather cleaner formulated for the leather type. Avoid soap, saddle soap, household cleaner, or baby wipes: they leave residues and can strip the finish. Apply the cleaner to a soft cloth (not directly to the leather), wipe in a small circular motion, and buff dry. For aniline leather, test any product on a hidden area first: some cleaners temporarily alter the colour.

Apply a leather conditioner after cleaning and every three to six months in a heated indoor environment (central heating dries leather faster than many people expect). Work the conditioner into the leather with a soft cloth using circular motions and allow it to absorb for 15 to 20 minutes before buffing off the surplus. A well-conditioned leather stays supple and develops a natural patina.

The first sign is a loss of softness: the leather feels stiffer and less pliable, especially at the seat edges and armrests. Small surface cracks appear next, usually along natural fold lines. At this stage the leather is still recoverable with regular conditioning over several months. Once the cracks deepen and extend across the surface, the structure is compromised and professional restoration or replacement is the realistic option.
